learning_objectives.text: Define fundamental programming concepts including variables, data types, and operators
Explain the purpose and behavior of control flow structures
Apply function definitions to solve programming problems
Implement exception handling for robust error management
Compare and contrast different data structures for specific use cases

questions.stem: Which of the following correctly defines a variable in Python?

distractor_rationale.A: Syntax from JavaScript - common confusion for students with prior JS experience
distractor_rationale.C: Syntax from statically-typed languages like C/Java - students may expect explicit type declaration
distractor_rationale.D: Syntax from JavaScript ES6 - common confusion for students with prior JS experience

questions.generation_rationale: Tests recognition of Python's dynamic typing and assignment syntax

questions.stem: What data type does Python assign to the value 3.14?

distractor_rationale.A: Confusion between integer and floating-point representation
distractor_rationale.C: Terminology from other languages (C, Java) - Python doesn't have a 'double' type
distractor_rationale.D: Confusion with Python's decimal module vs built-in float type

questions.generation_rationale: Tests recall of Python's numeric data types

questions.stem: Which statement best explains why Python uses indentation for code blocks?

options.text: To make the code more compact
To define the scope and structure of code blocks
To allow the interpreter to run faster
To maintain compatibility with other languages

distractor_rationale.A: Misconception that indentation is purely aesthetic
distractor_rationale.C: Misconception about indentation affecting runtime performance

questions.generation_rationale: Tests understanding of Python's syntactic design decisions

questions.stem: Explain what happens when a 'while' loop's condition is initially False.

options.text: The loop body executes once then stops

distractor_rationale.A: Confusion with do-while loop behavior from other languages
distractor_rationale.C: Misconception that false conditions cause errors
distractor_rationale.D: Confusion between false and infinite loop conditions

questions.generation_rationale: Tests comprehension of loop initialization behavior

questions.stem: You need to write a function that calculates the factorial of a number. Which function definition correctly implements this using recursion?

options.text: def factorial(n): if n <= 1: return 1; return n * factorial(n-1)

distractor_rationale.A: Missing base case - would cause infinite recursion
distractor_rationale.C: Missing base case - would cause infinite recursion (different order)
distractor_rationale.D: Not actually recursive - shows misunderstanding of recursion concept

questions.generation_rationale: Tests application of recursive function definition with base case

questions.stem: Given the requirement to process a list of temperatures and convert each from Celsius to Fahrenheit, which function signature is most appropriate?

options.text: def convert(celsius_list): return [c * 9/5 + 32 for c in celsius_list]

distractor_rationale.A: Doesn't accept parameters - can't process provided list
distractor_rationale.C: Only handles single value, not a list
distractor_rationale.D: Doesn't perform conversion - only prints input

questions.generation_rationale: Tests application of function parameters and list comprehension

questions.stem: You are writing code to read user input that should be converted to an integer. Which exception handling approach correctly catches invalid input?

options.text: try: num = int(input()) except ValueError: print('Invalid input')
try: num = int(input()) catch ValueError: print('Invalid')

distractor_rationale.A: Bare except clause - bad practice that catches all exceptions silently
distractor_rationale.C: Not using exception handling at all - would crash on invalid input
distractor_rationale.D: Wrong keyword - 'catch' is not Python syntax (from Java/C++)

questions.generation_rationale: Tests practical application of exception handling for input validation

questions.stem: Compare lists and tuples in Python. Which statement correctly describes a key difference?

options.text: Lists use square brackets while tuples use curly braces
Lists are mutable while tuples are immutable
Tuples can only contain strings while lists can contain any type
Lists are faster to iterate than tuples

distractor_rationale.A: Curly braces are for sets/dicts - syntax confusion
distractor_rationale.C: Both can contain any type - misconception about type restrictions
distractor_rationale.D: Actually tuples are slightly faster - performance misconception

questions.generation_rationale: Tests analysis of core differences between data structure types

questions.stem: Analyze which data structure would be most efficient for storing and retrieving user information by username.

options.text: A dictionary with username as key

distractor_rationale.A: Would require O(n) search time - inefficient for lookup
distractor_rationale.C: Would require complex indexing and O(n) search - inefficient
distractor_rationale.D: Sets only store values, not key-value pairs - can't store user info

questions.generation_rationale: Tests analysis of data structure efficiency for specific use cases

questions.stem: Describe the primary purpose of using a set instead of a list in Python.

options.text: To maintain insertion order of elements
To store unique elements and perform set operations

distractor_rationale.A: Lists maintain order; sets historically did not (though Python 3.7+ dicts do)
distractor_rationale.C: Opposite of set behavior - sets eliminate duplicates

questions.generation_rationale: Tests understanding of set data structure purpose and uniqueness property

feedback.message: Question stem is on the longer side but acceptable for scenario-based questions
